common_pests: Planting new ash trees is no longer recommended due to the trees' susceptibility to the emerald ash borer, a pest that feeds under the bark and bores into the wood.
Emerald ash borer will typically kill an ash tree within 3 to 5 years after infestation and, once infestation occurs, it is difficult to eradicate.
Other potential problems are redheaded ash borer, lilac borer, carpenter worm, oyster shell scale, leaf miners, fall webworms, ash sawflies, Ash Flower Gall Mite and ash leaf cur
care.used_for: ["The juice from leaves used on mosquito bites for relief of swelling and itching and are a prophylactic measure for snake bites. description: Fraxinus excelsior, known as the ash, or European ash or common ash to distinguish it from other types of ash, is a flowering plant species in the olive family Oleaceae. The northernmost location is in the Trondheimsfjord region of Norway.

description: Fraxinus pennsylvanica, the green ash or red ash, is a species of ash native to eastern and central North America, from Nova Scotia west to southeastern Alberta and eastern Colorado, south to northern Florida, and southwest to Oklahoma and eastern Texas. It has spread and become naturalized in much of the western United States and also in Argentina and Europe, from Spain to Russia.

common_problems: Potential disease problems include fungal leaf spots, powdery mildew, rust, anthracnose, cankers, and ash yellows.
General ash decline is also a concern.
Brittle branches are susceptible to damage from high winds and snow/ice.
See potential insect and disease problem factsheets
common_pests: Green ash trees are generally susceptible to several insect problems including the Emerald ash borer, lilac borer, carpenter worm, oyster shell scale, leaf miners, fall webworms, ash sawflies, ash leaf curl aphids.
See potential insect and disease problem factsheets

care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Cats, Toxic to Horses. Signs: Gastrointestinal upset (most common), incoordination, increased heart rate, death (rare).

Anthracnose, twig blight, cankers, powdery mildew and root rot are common disease problems.
Susceptible to copper deficiency.
Invasive.
See native or less problematic suggestions to the left.
common_pests: Whiteflies, Japanese and ligustrum weevils, white peach scale and privet rust mites are common insect problems.
care.toxicity_symptoms: Gastrointestinal upset (most common), incoordination, increased heart rate, death (rare).
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.

description: Ligustrum vulgare is a species of Ligustrum native to central and southern Europe, north Africa and southwestern Asia, from Ireland and southwestern Sweden south to Morocco, and east to Poland and northwestern Iran.
origin: Eurasia
synonyms: ["Ligustrum vulgare f. pendulum", "Ligustrum vulgare var. lactiflorum", "Ligustrum vulgare var. leucocarpum", "Ligustrum vulgare var. auriflorum", "Ligustrum vulgare subsp. italicum", "Ligustrum vulgare f. pyramidale", "Ligustrum vulgare var. aureum", "Ligustrum vulgare f. lodense", "Ligustrum italicum", "Ligustrum vulgare f. xanthocarpum"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Dogs, Toxic to Cats, Toxic to Horses. Signs: Drooling, abdominal pain, diarrhea, colic, depression
common_problems: Whiteflies, Japanese and ligustrum weevils, white peach scale, and privet rust mites are common insect problems.
Anthracnose, twig blight, cankers, powdery mildew, and root rot are common disease problems.
Susceptible to copper deficiency.
It can be weedy in disturbed areas and has escaped and naturalized in moist areas.
It is listed as invasive in North Carolina by the North Carolina Native Plant Society (calling it a  Significant Threat ).
common_pests: Whiteflies, Japanese and ligustrum weevils, white peach scale, and privet rust mites are common insect problems.
care.toxicity_symptoms: Drooling, abdominal pain, diarrhea, colic, depression
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.

description: The fruits are oval green drupes that measure 1.5 inches long.  They turn purplish-black when ripened and are edible. Each olive contains one pit.
origin: Mediterranean, Africa, and South Central China

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: The olive has no serious insect pests or diseases.
Monitor the tree for olive knot, verticillium wilt, and root rot.
common_pests: The olive has no serious insect pests or diseases.
Scale insects may be a problem.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) The common olive was one of the first trees to be cultivated and dates back to 2500 B.C. The olive branch is a universal symbol of peace. It is used for medicine, fuel, and food.

description: The olive is a species of subtropical evergreen tree in the family Oleaceae. Originating in Asia Minor, it is abundant throughout the Mediterranean Basin, with wild subspecies in Africa and western Asia; modern cultivars are traced primarily to the Near East, Aegean Sea, and Strait of Gibraltar. The olive is the type species for its genus, Olea, and lends its name to the Oleaceae plant family, which includes lilac, jasmine, forsythia, and ash.
origin: Mediterranean, Africa, and South Central China

description: Very fragrant smell like apricots, small white flowers in early spring and some in the fall appear in leaf axils. The flowers can be dried and added to tea.
origin: East Asia

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: It is frost-sensitive, and injury will affect flowering the following fall.
common_pests: Watch for aphids and scale.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) The flowers of this plant are used in teas and other beverages that are consumed in the Far East.  Extracts from the flower are highly valuable, and are used in some of the most expensive perfumes.

description: Ovoid, bluish purple-black to black drupe less than an inch in size that is seldom seen in cultivation.
origin: South Korea, Japan, and Taiwan

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: False Holly has no serious pest or disease problems, Observe for scale and aphids.
common_pests: False Holly has no serious pest or disease problems, Observe for scale and aphids.

description: Phillyrea angustifolia, the narrow-leaved mock privet, is a species of flowering plant in the olive family Oleaceae, native to the western and central Mediterranean.

Phillyrea latifolia, commonly known as green olive tree or mock privet, is a species of tree in the family Oleaceae. It is native to the Mediterranean Basin, from Morocco and Portugal in the west, to the Levant in the east.
Fragrant purple-pink  flowers arranged in dense, terminal clusters

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: 4-to-6-inch panicles (often in pairs) of lilac-purple flowers.
origin: Northeast China / Manchuria to Korea

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: The flower blooms are followed by clusters of warty, dry, oblong, or ovoid capsules. They measure about 0.75 inches long and are initially green and then turn yellow. The capsules can persist through the winter.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Brown capsules located at the terminal tips of branches
origin: Northern China and Mongolia, far eastern Russia

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: It is somewhat resistant to mildew, scales, and borers but is susceptible to blights, leaf spots, wilt, and ringspot virus.
common_pests: It is somewhat resistant to mildew, scales, and borers but is susceptible to blights, leaf spots, wilt, and ringspot virus.
Insect pests include scales, borers, leaf miners and caterpillars.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Leaves and fruit are used to make a tonic for the treatment of sore throats.

description: Syringa vulgaris, the lilac or common lilac, is a species of flowering plant in the olive family, Oleaceae. Native to the Balkan Peninsula, it is widely cultivated for its scented flowers in Europe and North America.
origin: Balkan Peninsula

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: Powdery mildew can seriously affect the appearance of the foliage in the summer but does little permanent damage to the shrub.
Prune the center of the plant to help with the airflow.
Additional disease problems include blights, leaf spots, wilts, ringspot virus, and honey fungus.
common_pests: Insect pests include scales, borers, leaf miners, thrips, and caterpillars.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) An essential oil obtained from the flowers can be used to make perfume. Green and brown dyes can be obtained from the leaves and a green dye from the flowers.
care.edible_parts: Flowers can be eaten raw or folded into a batter and fried to make fritters.
